title: Chocolate florentines
subtitle: Thin caramel, nut, and dried fruit biscuits with a chocolate base
excerpt: A buttery caramel base full of flaked almonds, dried cherries, and citrus peel, baked until lacy and golden, then cooled flat and coated on one side with dark chocolate.
sourceNotes: Florentines are documented in French and Italian patisserie traditions from the nineteenth century, with the name suggesting a Florentine origin though they are more closely associated with Austrian and Alsatian confectionery. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), Project Gutenberg, documents them as a standard confection in the French patisserie repertoire. The chocolate-backed version became the standard British interpretation in the twentieth century.

content.text: Florentines are a caramel-and-nut confection that sits at the boundary of biscuit and sweet. The base is a thin layer of caramel made with butter, cream, and sugar, set with a little flour and loaded with flaked almonds, dried cherries, and orange peel. The caramel spreads and bubbles in the oven, finding its own level before setting into a lacy, frangible disc. The underside is then coated with

The main technical challenge is keeping the caramel at the right consistency: too thin and the biscuits spread into each other; too stiff and they won't be lacy. Cooking the caramel until it reads 112 to 115°C on a probe thermometer gives a reliable result. Watch the oven closely; florentines go from lacy and golden to burnt in under two minutes at the end of baking.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan (200°C conventional). Melt {{unsalted-butter}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{double-cream}} together in a saucepan over medium heat, stirring until smooth. Bring to the boil and cook to 112°C on a probe thermometer; this takes about three minutes. Remove from the heat and stir in {{plain-flour}}, {{blanched-almonds}}, {{dried-currants}}, and the zest from {{orange}}.

Drop teaspoons of mixture well apart on a baking-paper-lined tray; they spread significantly. Bake for 8 to 10 minutes until the edges are deep golden-brown and the centres are lacy and set. They will firm as they cool; do not try to move them until they are completely cold. If any spread into each other, trim with a sharp knife while still just warm.

Melt {{dark-chocolate}} in a heatproof bowl over a pan of barely simmering water, stirring until smooth. Remove from the heat and allow to cool to about 31°C. It should feel just cooler than body temperature. Spread a thin layer over the flat side of each cooled florentine. Set chocolate-side up on the rack. Draw a fork across the surface in a zigzag pattern before it sets, if you like. Leave to set fully at room temperature.
The caramel mixture can be made and refrigerated for up to two days before baking. It thickens in the fridge; warm it gently to loosen before spooning onto trays.
For a more traditional version: use pistachios and dried sour cherries in place of almonds and currants.
Milk chocolate coating makes a sweeter florentine; dark chocolate at 70% gives a better contrast with the caramel and fruit.
